text: After this, Absalom got himself a chariot, horses, and fifty men to run ahead of him.

He would rise early and stand beside the road leading to the city gate, and whenever someone came with a dispute to bring before the king for a ruling, Absalom would call out and ask which tribe the man was from.
Absalom would tell him his case sounded good and fair, but that the king had no one appointed to hear it.
He would add that he wished he were made judge in the land, so that anyone with a complaint could come to him and get justice.
And whenever someone came near to bow to him, he would reach out, take hold of the man, and kiss him instead.
Absalom treated everyone who came to the king for judgment this way, and in doing so won over the loyalty of the people of Israel.
After four years had passed, Absalom asked the king for permission to go to Hebron to fulfill a vow he claimed he had made to the LORD.
He explained that while living in exile in Geshur he had vowed that if the LORD ever brought him back to Jerusalem, he would worship him there.
The king told him to go in peace, and Absalom set out for Hebron.
But Absalom sent secret messengers to every tribe of Israel, instructing them that as soon as they heard the trumpet sound, they should declare that Absalom had become king in Hebron.
Two hundred men from Jerusalem went along with Absalom as invited guests, but they went innocently, knowing nothing of the plot.
While offering sacrifices, Absalom also sent for Ahithophel, David's counselor, from his hometown of Giloh, and the conspiracy grew stronger as more and more people joined Absalom.
A messenger came to David and told him that the loyalty of Israel had shifted to Absalom.
David told all his officials in Jerusalem that they needed to flee at once, or none of them would escape Absalom, warning that he would move quickly and bring disaster on the city.
The king's officials answered that they were ready to do whatever the king decided.
So the king set out on foot with his whole household following him, leaving behind ten concubines to look after the palace.
The king and all his people went out and stopped at the last house on the way.
All his men passed by him — his guard of Cherethites and Pelethites, along with six hundred men from Gath who had followed him there — marching ahead of the king.
The king said to Ittai the Gittite, why should he come along with them, since he was a foreigner and an exile from his own homeland, and should instead go back and stay with the new king.

David pointed out that Ittai had only just arrived, and it wasn't right to drag him into wandering with no clear destination; he told him to take his kinsmen and go back, wishing him steadfast love and faithfulness.
But Ittai answered that as surely as the LORD lived and the king lived, wherever the king went, whether to death or to life, that is where his servant would be also.
David told Ittai to go on then, and Ittai passed on ahead with all his men and their families.
The whole countryside wept loudly as the people passed by, and the king crossed the Kidron valley with everyone continuing on toward the wilderness.
Zadok and Abiathar, along with the Levites, came carrying the ark of the covenant of God, and they set it down until everyone had finished leaving the city.
The king told Zadok to take the ark back into the city, saying that if he found favor with the LORD, he would be brought back to see it and its resting place again.
But if the LORD had no pleasure in him, David said he was ready for God to do with him whatever seemed good.
The king also told Zadok, as a seer, to go back to the city in peace along with his son Ahimaaz and Abiathar's son Jonathan.
David said he would wait at the fords of the wilderness until word reached him from them.
So Zadok and Abiathar carried the ark of God back to Jerusalem and stayed there.
David climbed the ascent of the Mount of Olives, weeping the whole way, walking barefoot with his head covered, and everyone with him did the same, weeping as they climbed.
When David was told that Ahithophel had joined Absalom's conspiracy, he prayed that the LORD would turn Ahithophel's advice into foolishness.
When David reached the summit where God was worshiped, Hushai the Archite met him with his robe torn and dust on his head.
David told him that if he came along, he would only be a burden.
But if he went back to the city and offered to serve Absalom the way he had served David before, he could help undermine Ahithophel's counsel.
David reminded him that the priests Zadok and Abiathar would be there too, so he should report anything he heard from the palace to them.
Their two sons, Ahimaaz and Jonathan, would also be there, and could be used to relay everything back to David.
So Hushai, David's friend, went into the city just as Absalom was arriving in Jerusalem.
